title: Spoken Kannada Kids Curriculum Proposal
purpose: A reviewable replacement/overlay curriculum for children learning spoken Kannada from English.
design_notes: Start with high-frequency real-life classroom and home phrases, not adult phrasebook topics.
Use short call-and-response exchanges that a child can act out immediately.
Keep each chapter focused on one communicative goal and a small reusable vocabulary set.
Prefer natural spoken Kannada over literal English translation.
Include Kannada script, learner-friendly romanization, and original English intent for each line.
Dialogues should sound like real kids talking, not like grammar worksheets.
Each conversation still carries concepts_taught so we know what language idea it practices.
Tense practice should appear inside scenes: food, games, car rides, school, parks, and playdates.

strengths: The source has many paired English/Kannada lines and broad everyday topics.
It already contains useful categories such as introductions, home, food, health, cinema, shopping, and travel.
The side-by-side format works well for visual sentence practice.
issues_for_kids: The first chapter jumps into third-person references like he/she/this person/that person before children have enough personal survival phrases.
Many scenarios are adult-heavy: rent, lawyers, office situations, formal visitors, and old-fashioned hotel dialogue.
Some English prompts are unnatural for children today, for example 'How do you do?' or overly formal 'be seated'.
Several OCR romanizations are noisy, so they are not safe as practice targets without correction.
The chapters are long; kids usually need smaller scenes with repetition, choice, and familiar settings.

chapters.title: Ice Cream After School: greetings, names, flavors
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Kids start with a simple after-school ice cream story: hi, names, choosing flavors, saying it was good, and goodbye.

conversations.title: Meeting Meena
conversations.setting: near an ice cream shop after school
conversations.goal: Say hi, ask if someone is in your class, and exchange names.

lines.speaker: Raju
lines.kannada: ಮೀನಾ, ನಿಮ್ಮನ್ನು ಭೇಟಿ ಮಾಡಿ ಸಂತೋಷ ಆಯ್ತು.
lines.roman: Meena, nimmannu bheti maadi santosha aaytu.

conversations.title: Choosing Ice Cream
conversations.goal: Ask for ice cream and talk about mango and chocolate flavors.

lines.speaker: Raju
lines.roman: Nimage yaava flavor ishta anta helteera?

conversations.title: After Ice Cream
conversations.setting: outside the shop after eating ice cream
conversations.goal: Say something was good and make a simple tomorrow plan.

chapters.title: Recess Plans: feelings, choices, permission
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Feelings become more engaging when they connect to games, snacks, and play plans.

conversations.title: Choosing a game at recess
conversations.goal: Talk about mood through a real play plan

conversations.title: Taking a break after playing
conversations.goal: Say tired, ask for water, and decide what happens next

chapters.title: Family Car Drive: family, he/she/we, future plans
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Family introductions are more memorable when tied to a weekend car ride or fun outing.

conversations.title: Family photo before a car drive
conversations.goal: Introduce family and talk about a weekend plan

lines.speaker: Kiran
lines.english: Yes, we’re going for a car drive.

conversations.title: Everyone wants to come
conversations.goal: Use he/she/we while planning to go out

lines.speaker: Maya
lines.english: Great, we can all play after lunch.

chapters.title: Eating Basics: eat, ate, done, okay
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Kids hear food check-ins every day, so this chapter gives them survival phrases they can memorize quickly.

conversations.title: Come eat now
conversations.goal: Understand common home eating commands and responses.

conversations.title: Did you eat?
conversations.goal: Answer common food check-in questions.

conversations.title: Are you done eating?
conversations.goal: Say done/not done and ask for water.

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: Yes, I’m okay. Can I have water?

chapters.title: Household Helpers: bring this, take that
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Bring/take/give/put are everyday commands children hear constantly at home.

conversations.title: Bring the plate
conversations.goal: Practice bring/take/give with household objects.

lines.speaker: Mom
lines.english: Now take this cup to dad.

conversations.title: Put it there
conversations.goal: Use put/keep/open/close naturally.

conversations.title: Give it to grandma
conversations.goal: Practice respectful giving to elders.

lines.speaker: Mom
lines.english: Now give one to your brother.

chapters.title: Classroom Word Game: listen, repeat, ask again
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Classroom survival phrases are easier when the scene includes a game and a friendly teacher.

conversations.title: Teacher explains a word game
conversations.goal: Follow classroom directions in a fun game

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.english: Say your name and one food you like.

conversations.title: Say it one more time
conversations.goal: Ask for repetition without feeling embarrassed

chapters.title: Toys and Game Board: this/that, here/there, things
chapters.why_this_helps_kids: Object words should start with things kids care about: toys, books, pencils, and games.

conversations.title: Finding the game board
conversations.goal: Use this/that with toys and games

lines.speaker: Child
lines.english: That’s my Snake and Ladder board.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.english: Do you want to play now?

conversations.title: Finding crayons before drawing
conversations.goal: Ask where things are using here/there/under

lines.speaker: Nila
lines.english: Oh, thanks. Where’s the red crayon?

chapters.title: Getting Around: car, bus, airplane

chapters.title: Yesterday Chapter: everything already happened

conversations.title: Yesterday at the park

lines.speaker: Maya
lines.english: Good. Past, now, and later all in one story!

grammar_spiral.purpose: Repeat a small set of grammar ideas through fun kid conversations instead of teaching grammar as isolated rules.

targets.label: Future / later
examples: We will go to the park.

targets.label: Here / there / this / that
targets.kid_prompt: Point and say where it is.

targets.label: I / you / he / she / we / they
People / places / things
examples: The candy is on the table.

grammar_spiral.spiral_rule: Every 2-3 chapters should include at least one line that contrasts now, before, and later, or here/there, or he/she/they.

source_coverage_policy.goal: Teach the same breadth of language from the original source, but through kid-natural conversations.

reframe_examples.rent/house ownership: my room, friend’s house, big/small rooms, where toys are kept
reframe_examples.office leave/messages: class attendance, teacher message, friend has not come yet
reframe_examples.hotel/town: ice cream shop, pasta place, kid-friendly restaurant
reframe_examples.smoking health advice: too much candy or screen time is not good
reframe_examples.college subjects/admission: school subjects, favorite class, joining a club/team
reframe_examples.tailor/shirt conditionals: party clothes, forgot game board, if you told me yesterday...
reframe_examples.farm/crops: garden, fruit trees, mango/coconut, watering plants
reframe_examples.cinema: movie night, cartoon, theme park show
reframe_examples.travel to Delhi: car drive, bus ride, zoo, Disneyland/theme park, grandma’s house

chapter_groups.purpose: Names, friends, food check-ins, household commands, family, classroom survival, this/that/here/there.
Transport, measurements, routines, clothes, health, weather, animals, shopping, directions, books, stories, festivals.
Single-tense chapters first, then same-sentence tense drills, then mixed stories.
Permission, requests, helping, elders/teachers vs friends/younger kids.

tense_spiral_plan.simple_pattern: yesterday / today-now / tomorrow, then mixed stories
tense_spiral_plan.goal: Kids first hear one tense repeatedly in isolation, then compare the same idea across past, present, and future.

speech_level_plan.goal: Teach that Kannada changes depending on whether you talk to elders/teachers respectfully or to friends/younger kids casually.
